summary: Patrick McKenzie presents a comprehensive guide to selling software to businesses, covering low-touch, high-touch, and hybrid sales models. He argues that SaaS companies should price based on customer perceived value rather than cost, use tiered pricing with strategic plan names, and offer annual billing to improve cash flow and reduce churn. He demonstrates how product tours, lifecycle email campaigns, and drip sequences can dramatically improve conversion rates. He also shares how he personally won enterprise hospital contracts over an 800-pound gorilla competitor by investing time in relationships, and teaches specific tactics for navigating purchasing departments and enterprise sales cycles.
key_points: Price based on customer perceived value, not cost — an HVAC company pays $200/month for $1.96 worth of phone calls because the business value is enormous
Metered billing is an anti-pattern for low-touch SaaS because customers have a high perceived cost associated with uncertainty and will overpay for predictability
Product tours that demonstrate one awesome improvement in the first five minutes can reduce first-day abandonment from 60% and increase sales by 30%
Annual billing offered via email to existing customers with a small discount can convert 10-25% of monthly customers, generating massive upfront cash flow
Enterprise sales can be won by small operators who invest personal attention — the magic answer to almost any enterprise request is 'I can do that with a one-year commitment'
money_quotes: Your customers do not perceive the value of your applications as being dumb telephone pipes. If they wanted dumb telephone pipes, they could get them from AT&T for like $14 a month.
It cost me $1.96 to make those phone calls for him for last month, but I'm putting his daughter through Harvard.
Customers have a high perceived cost associated with uncertainty.
People hate uncertainty so much that they will overpay to never have to worry about it.
You are not taking your customer's money. You are taking the business's money. No customer feels a pain in their life.
If I'm going to submit this to my boss and it says 'hobbyist,' we're an enterprise. We need the enterprise plan.
You don't understand. It's his baby. You take care of your baby. If we ever have a problem with it, we know who we're going to be talking to.

Never give people a break on the unit price, because the purchasing department is only scored against whether they get the purchase in at lower than the best unit price that they have ever seen or gotten from you.
key_insight: Software companies massively underprice by anchoring to their costs rather than to the business value customers receive, and the hybrid sales model — combining low-touch self-serve with high-touch enterprise engagement — is the most underexploited opportunity in SaaS.
